title: The Modern LLM Era: Prompting, RAG & Agents

lessons.title: What an LLM Is: Predicting the Next Word
lessons.content:
You have met the **transformer** (the network design that looks at all words at once using **attention**, deciding which words matter most). A **Large Language Model (LLM)** is simply a *giant* transformer, and it is the engine behind chat assistants like the ones you already use. This lesson is about the one deceptively simple trick that makes it work.

**Hook.** Think of the autocomplete on your phone that suggests the next word as you type. Now imagine an autocomplete that has read much of the internet, so its suggestions are astonishingly good. That, in one sentence, is an LLM: a **super-powered autocomplete**.

**Build, the core trick.** An LLM is trained on huge amounts of text with one goal: **predict the next word**. First we need a word for the pieces of text it handles: a **token** is a chunk of text, roughly a word or a part of a word, the unit the model reads and writes. The model looks at the text so far and guesses the most likely next token. To write a whole reply, it predicts one token, **adds it to the text, and predicts again**, over and over. This loop has a name, **autoregression**, but the idea is just: next token, then the next, then the next.

Why does such a simple goal produce something so capable? Because to predict the next token *well*, across billions of sentences, the model has to absorb grammar, facts, reasoning patterns, and style. The answer for each guess is simply **the actual next word in the text**, so the text teaches itself, no humans labeling anything. That is called **self-supervised** learning, and because it needs no labels, it **scales** to enormous amounts of text.

The billions of internal numbers it adjusts during training are its **parameters** (the learned weights). More parameters, more text, and more computing power tend to make a stronger model.

**Honest note.** Because an LLM only predicts *plausible* text, it can be confidently **wrong**. A made-up but fluent answer is called a **hallucination**. The model has a **knowledge cutoff** (it only knows text up to when it was trained) and no built-in fact-checker.

**Recap.** An LLM is a giant transformer trained to predict the next token, looping that prediction to write text. The training labels come free from the text itself (self-supervised), which is why it scales, but it can still hallucinate.

items.prompt: What is the core training objective of a Large Language Model (LLM)?
choices: To predict the next word (token) in the text, over and over
To look up the correct answer in a built-in database of facts
To memorize every webpage word for word and store it
To translate everything it reads into a secret code
items.explanation: An LLM is trained with one deceptively simple goal: predict the next token given the text so far. It does not consult a stored database of answers, it does not memorize pages verbatim, and it does not encode text into a code, it learns to guess what comes next, and doing that well forces it to absorb language and facts.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is a 'token'?
choices: A password that unlocks the model
A piece of text, roughly a word or part of a word, that the model reads and writes
The final answer the model gives
A picture the model draws
items.explanation: A token is just a chunk of text, about a word or a word-piece, the unit an LLM processes. It is not a password, not the final answer, and not an image, it is the small building block of the text itself.

items.prompt: How does an LLM generate a whole sentence rather than just one word?
choices: It writes the entire sentence in a single step with no repetition
It copies a full sentence from its training data
It asks a human to finish the sentence for it
It predicts one token, adds it to the text, and predicts the next, repeating the loop (autoregression)
items.explanation: Generation is the next-token prediction repeated: predict a token, append it, predict again, which is called autoregression. It does not produce a whole sentence at once, copy stored sentences, or hand off to a human, it loops one token at a time.

items.prompt: Predicting the next word is called 'self-supervised' learning. Why does that label fit?
choices: Because a team of humans labels every sentence with the right answer
Because the model supervises the humans who built it
Because the answer for each guess is simply the actual next word in the text, so no human labels are needed
Because it only works on data that has already been hand-graded
items.explanation: It is self-supervised because the text provides its own answer, the real next word, so no separate human labeling is required. It is precisely the absence of hand-labeling that distinguishes it, which is also why it scales to massive text.

items.prompt: The everyday analogy for an LLM offered here is...
choices: A calculator that only does arithmetic
A search engine that returns a list of links
A super-powered autocomplete that has read much of the internet
A filing cabinet that stores documents exactly
items.explanation: An LLM is like autocomplete on your phone, but trained on a huge amount of text so its next-word guesses are remarkably good. It is not a plain calculator, a link-listing search engine, or a literal filing cabinet of stored documents.

items.prompt: What are an LLM's 'parameters'?
choices: The questions a user types in
The rules a programmer wrote by hand for each topic
The web links it cites in an answer
The billions of internal numbers (weights) it adjusts during training
items.explanation: Parameters are the model's learned weights, often billions of internal numbers tuned during training. They are not the user's questions, hand-written rules, or citations, more parameters (with more data and compute) tend to make a stronger model.

items.prompt: What is a 'hallucination' from an LLM, and why do they happen?
choices: A deliberate joke the model is programmed to tell
A made-up but fluent answer; it happens because the model predicts plausible text rather than checking truth
A message warning the user that the answer is uncertain
A picture the model imagines instead of replying
items.explanation: A hallucination is a confident, fluent answer that is simply wrong, and it arises because the model is built to produce plausible-sounding text, not to verify facts. It is not an intentional joke, a built-in uncertainty warning, or an image, which is exactly why important outputs need checking.

items.prompt: What does it mean that an LLM has a 'knowledge cutoff'?
choices: It only knows about text up to the point when it was trained, not later events
It can only answer a fixed number of questions per day
It refuses to discuss any difficult topic
It cuts off its answers after one sentence
items.explanation: A knowledge cutoff means the model's information stops at its training time, so it does not inherently know events after that. It is not a daily question limit, a topic refusal, or a one-sentence cap, it simply has not seen newer text.

items.prompt: Why does training bigger models on more text with more computing power tend to help (the 'scaling' story)?
choices: Because larger models simply store more saved answers to retrieve
Because bigger models stop needing any training data
Because to predict the next word better across enormous text, the model must absorb more grammar, facts, and reasoning patterns
Because size guarantees the model is always correct
items.explanation: Scaling helps because squeezing out better next-word predictions over vast text pushes the model to capture more language structure, facts, and reasoning. It is not about storing retrievable answers, it still needs lots of data, and bigger never guarantees correctness, hallucinations remain.

items.prompt: A friend says, 'When you ask an LLM a question, it looks the answer up in a database of facts.' What is the better view?
choices: They are right; an LLM is basically a search of a fact database
An LLM only repeats sentences it copied word for word
An LLM cannot answer questions at all
An LLM generates an answer by predicting likely next tokens from patterns it learned; it is not looking answers up in a stored database by default
items.explanation: By default an LLM produces text by predicting plausible next tokens from learned patterns, not by querying a database of stored facts. That is also why it can be fluently wrong, it is generating, not retrieving, unless it is specifically connected to outside sources.

lessons.title: From Raw Model to Helpful Assistant, and How to Prompt It
lessons.content:
A freshly trained next-word predictor is powerful but raw: it has read the internet, yet it has not learned how to be a polite, helpful *assistant*. This lesson covers the two steps that fix that, and how you, the user, steer the result with your words.

**Hook.** Imagine a brilliant generalist who has read nearly everything but has never had a job. They know a lot, yet they do not yet know how to give a clear, helpful, on-topic answer. They need training and coaching. An LLM is the same.

**Build, two training stages.** The first stage is **pretraining**: read huge amounts of internet text and learn to predict the next token (from the last lesson). The result is a raw model that knows language but not manners. The second stage turns it into an assistant. In **fine-tuning** (also called instruction-tuning) the model is trained further on examples of good, helpful answers so it learns to *follow instructions*. Then comes **learning from human feedback**, known as **RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)**, which in plain words means: people compare and rate the model's answers, and the model is nudged toward replies that are **helpful, honest, and harmless**. Note the order: **pretraining learns language; fine-tuning and RLHF teach behavior**. They are different steps, do not mix them up.

**Build, prompting.** Because you steer an LLM entirely with words, **how you ask matters**. The message you give it is called a **prompt**. Three habits reliably improve answers: give **clear instructions** (say exactly what you want), include an **example or two in the prompt** (called **few-shot**, because the model learns the pattern from those shots), and ask it to **think step by step** for harder problems. A caution: a **longer prompt is not automatically better**, padding it with extra words can even hurt, what helps is *clarity*, not length.

**Build, the context window.** An LLM can only consider a limited amount of text at once, called the **context window**. Think of it as its short-term memory, a window over the recent text. Anything that scrolls out of that window is **forgotten**. That is why a very long chat can lose track of something said far earlier.

**Recap.** Pretraining teaches language; fine-tuning and RLHF turn the raw model into a helpful assistant. You steer it with prompts, clear asks, a few examples, and step-by-step requests, all within its context window, the short-term memory that bounds what it can see.

items.prompt: What do fine-tuning and learning from human feedback (RLHF) turn a raw pretrained model into?
choices: A bigger database of stored web pages
A model that no longer understands language
A search engine that returns links
A helpful chat assistant that follows instructions and aims to be helpful, honest, and harmless
items.explanation: Fine-tuning plus RLHF shapes a raw next-word predictor into a polite, instruction-following assistant nudged toward helpful, honest, harmless answers. It does not become a web-page database or a link-returning search engine, and it certainly does not lose its language ability, it gains better behavior.

items.prompt: What is the difference between pretraining and fine-tuning?
choices: They are two names for exactly the same step
Pretraining learns language from huge internet text; fine-tuning then adapts the model's behavior, teaching it to follow instructions helpfully
Fine-tuning happens first, then pretraining replaces it
Pretraining is done by users typing prompts, fine-tuning is done by the model alone
items.explanation: Pretraining is the first stage, learning language by predicting the next token over vast text, while fine-tuning is a later stage that shapes helpful, instruction-following behavior. They are distinct steps in a fixed order (pretraining first), not the same thing, not reversed, and neither is just users typing prompts.

items.prompt: In plain words, what is RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback)?
choices: People rate the model's answers, and the model is nudged toward helpful, honest, harmless replies
The model retrains itself on the entire internet again
Humans manually write every answer the model will ever give
The model is shrunk to run on a phone
items.explanation: RLHF means humans judge the model's responses and that feedback steers it toward better, safer, more helpful answers. It is not a fresh full retraining on the internet, not humans writing every reply by hand, and not a size-reduction technique.

items.prompt: The analogy used for turning a raw model into an assistant is best described as...
choices: A calculator getting a new battery
A library being moved to a new building
A brilliant generalist who has read everything, then gets job training and coaching on how to be helpful and polite
A phone being charged overnight
items.explanation: The raw model is like a well-read generalist who still needs job training and coaching, exactly what fine-tuning and RLHF provide. The other options (a battery, a move, a charge) do not capture learning how to behave helpfully.

items.prompt: Which set of habits tends to improve the quality of an LLM's answer?
choices: Vague wording, no examples, and forbidding any reasoning
Typing in all capital letters and nothing else
Clear instructions, an example or two in the prompt (few-shot), and asking it to think step by step
Asking the same question many times quickly
items.explanation: Clear instructions, a few worked examples (few-shot), and a step-by-step request reliably help, because you steer the model with words. Vagueness, shouting in capitals, or repeating the question do not provide the clarity and structure that improve answers.

items.prompt: What does 'few-shot' prompting mean?
choices: Giving the model only a few seconds to answer
Asking only very short questions
Limiting the model to a few words in its reply
Including a small number of examples in the prompt so the model picks up the pattern you want
items.explanation: Few-shot means putting a few examples (shots) of the desired input-output pattern right into the prompt, which guides the model. It is not about time limits, short questions, or capping the reply length, it is about showing examples.

items.prompt: A friend insists, 'The longer and more stuffed my prompt is, the better the answer will always be.' Why is that misleading?
choices: It is completely true; more words always means a better answer
What helps is clarity, not raw length; padding a prompt with extra words can even make the answer worse
Prompts have no effect on the answer at all
Only one-word prompts ever work
items.explanation: A bigger prompt is not automatically better, clear, well-structured instructions help, while needless padding can confuse the model. Prompts clearly do matter (so they are not irrelevant), and useful prompts are certainly not limited to one word, the key is clarity over length.

items.prompt: What is the 'context window' of an LLM?
choices: The limited amount of text it can consider at once, like a short-term memory; text beyond it is forgotten
The pop-up window where you type your question
The model's permanent, unlimited memory of every chat ever
The window of time during which the model is awake
items.explanation: The context window is how much text the model can take into account at one time, its short-term memory, and anything outside that window is not seen. It is not the typing box on screen, not an unlimited permanent memory, and not a span of time.

items.prompt: Why might an LLM seem to 'forget' something you told it much earlier in a very long conversation?
choices: Because it deliberately ignores polite users
Because it deletes its training data as you chat
Because longer chats make the model physically smaller
Because that earlier text has scrolled out of its context window, the limited span of text it can consider at once
items.explanation: Once text falls outside the context window, the model can no longer see it, so it appears to forget. It is not snubbing the user, erasing its training, or shrinking, it simply has a bounded short-term memory.

items.prompt: Put the journey in the right order: how does a raw model become a helpful assistant you can steer?
choices: Fine-tuning/RLHF for behavior first, then pretraining on language, then prompting
Prompting first, which trains the model, then pretraining, then fine-tuning
Pretraining learns language, then fine-tuning/RLHF teaches helpful behavior, and then you steer the assistant with prompts (within its context window)
Only pretraining is needed; fine-tuning and prompting do nothing
items.explanation: The correct sequence is pretraining (learn language) then fine-tuning/RLHF (learn helpful behavior), after which you guide the finished assistant with prompts inside its context window. The other orders scramble the stages or wrongly claim fine-tuning and prompting are pointless.

lessons.title: RAG: Giving the Model an Open Book
lessons.content:
You know an LLM answers from patterns it learned during training, which means it can be out of date (knowledge cutoff) and can **hallucinate** confident but wrong facts. This lesson covers the standard fix: let the model **look things up**.

**Hook.** Picture two kinds of exam. In a closed-book exam you answer purely from memory, and if you misremember, you are simply wrong. In an **open-book exam** you can look up the relevant page first, then answer from it. **RAG turns the LLM's closed-book exam into an open-book one.**

**Build, what RAG is.** **RAG** stands for **Retrieval-Augmented Generation**. The word *retrieval* means *fetching* relevant information; *generation* is the model writing the answer. The flow is: take the user's **question**, **search your own documents** (your files, notes, or a company database) for the few passages most relevant to it, and **put those passages into the prompt** alongside the question. Now the model writes its answer using that real, current text in front of it, so the reply is **grounded** in actual sources instead of fuzzy memory.

**Build, why it helps.** RAG fixes outdated or unknown facts, the model can answer about things added to your documents *after* its training, and it **reduces hallucination**, because the relevant facts are supplied right there in the prompt rather than recalled from memory. This is exactly why companies build chatbots over their **own** documents with RAG: a support bot can answer from the current product manual, a policy bot from the latest handbook.

**Crucial point, RAG does NOT retrain the model.** This is the single most common misunderstanding. RAG changes **what goes into the prompt**, it does not change the model's weights or teach it anything permanently. The weights are untouched; you are simply handing the model the right pages to read each time. (Retraining, by contrast, would mean updating the model's parameters, which RAG never does.)

**Recap.** RAG retrieves relevant documents and stuffs them into the prompt so the model answers from real, current sources, an open-book exam. It grounds answers and cuts hallucination, and it works by feeding the model information at question time, not by retraining it.

items.prompt: What does RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) actually do?
choices: It retrains the model's weights on your documents every time you ask
It makes the model's answers longer regardless of content
It deletes the model's training so it starts fresh
It fetches relevant documents and puts them into the prompt, so the model answers from real, current sources
items.explanation: RAG retrieves the most relevant passages from your documents and inserts them into the prompt, grounding the answer in real text. It does not retrain or reset the model's weights, and it is about supplying facts, not about padding the answer's length.

items.prompt: Does RAG retrain or change the model's weights?
choices: Yes, RAG updates the model's parameters every query
No, RAG only changes what goes into the prompt; the model's weights are untouched
Yes, RAG permanently teaches the model new facts forever
Yes, RAG replaces the model with a new one each time
items.explanation: RAG leaves the weights alone, it simply provides relevant text in the prompt at question time. It does not update parameters, teach permanent new facts, or swap the model, which is the most common misconception to avoid.

items.prompt: Which everyday situation best captures the idea of RAG?
choices: An open-book exam, where you look up the relevant page and then answer from it
A closed-book exam answered purely from memory
A timed sprint with no thinking allowed
A multiple-choice test with random guessing
items.explanation: RAG is the open-book exam: the model looks up relevant material first, then answers from it. A closed-book, memory-only approach is the un-augmented model, and sprinting or random guessing do not capture retrieving real sources before answering.

items.prompt: How does RAG help reduce hallucinations?
choices: By forbidding the model from ever answering
By making the model guess faster
By supplying the relevant, real facts directly in the prompt, so the model answers from those instead of fuzzy memory
By hiding the question from the model
items.explanation: When the actual relevant text sits in the prompt, the model can ground its answer in it rather than recalling possibly wrong facts, which cuts hallucination. It does not work by silencing the model, rushing it, or hiding the question.

items.prompt: Why do companies often use RAG to build chatbots over their own documents?
choices: Because it lets the bot answer from their current, real documents (like the latest manual or handbook) without retraining the model
Because it makes the model forget all its training
Because it guarantees the bot can never be wrong about anything
Because it turns the chatbot into a search engine that only returns links
items.explanation: RAG lets a bot pull answers from a company's up-to-date documents at question time, no retraining needed, which is ideal for support or policy bots. It does not erase the model's training, it does not guarantee perfection, and it produces written answers, not just link lists.

items.prompt: What are the steps of a RAG flow, in order?
choices: Retrain the model, then ask the question, then delete the documents
Ask the question, ignore all documents, then guess from memory
Shrink the model, then search the web, then stop
Take the question, retrieve relevant documents, put them into the prompt, then the model writes a grounded answer
items.explanation: RAG goes question to retrieval to stuffing the documents into the prompt to a grounded answer. It does not retrain or delete anything, and the whole point is to use the retrieved documents rather than ignore them and guess.

items.prompt: An LLM trained last year is asked about a policy your company added last week. Plain (un-augmented) it gets it wrong. Why can RAG fix this?
choices: RAG retrains the model overnight on the new policy
RAG makes the model's knowledge cutoff disappear permanently
RAG can retrieve the new policy document and place it in the prompt, so the model answers from current text despite its older knowledge cutoff
RAG cannot help with anything newer than the model's training
items.explanation: RAG sidesteps the knowledge cutoff by fetching the new document and putting it in the prompt, so the model reads current information at answer time, no weight changes. It does not retrain the model, it does not permanently move the cutoff, and it is precisely useful for facts newer than training.

items.prompt: A teammate says, 'We use RAG, so our model has now learned all our documents by heart.' What is the accurate correction?
choices: RAG does not put anything into the model's memory; it fetches relevant documents into the prompt at question time, leaving the weights unchanged
They are right; RAG memorizes the documents into the model's weights
RAG deletes the documents after one use so nothing is learned
RAG only works if you first retrain the model on the documents
items.explanation: RAG never writes facts into the model's weights, it supplies relevant text in the prompt each time you ask, and the model's parameters stay the same. So nothing is learned by heart, the documents are not destroyed, and no prior retraining is required.

items.prompt: In the name 'Retrieval-Augmented Generation,' what does 'retrieval' refer to?
choices: The model deleting old answers
Generating brand-new training data
Shrinking the model for a phone
Fetching relevant information (documents or passages) to use when answering
items.explanation: Retrieval means fetching the relevant documents or passages so the generation step can use them. It is not deletion, data generation, or model compression, it is the look-up that makes the answer open-book.

items.prompt: What is the single best one-line summary of RAG?
choices: Retrain the model on your data so it memorizes everything
Make the model's answers as long as possible
Fetch relevant documents into the prompt so the model answers from real, current sources, an open book, without changing the model's weights
Replace the model with a plain keyword search
items.explanation: RAG is best summed up as feeding relevant documents into the prompt for a grounded, current answer, like an open-book exam, with no retraining of the model. It is not memorizing via retraining, not padding answers, and not a plain keyword search replacing the model.

lessons.title: Agents: When an LLM Can Use Tools and Act
lessons.content:
So far the LLM only *talks*, it reads a prompt and writes a reply. This final lesson is about giving it the ability to *act*: to use **tools** and take multiple steps toward a goal. That upgrade turns a chatbot into an **agent**.

**Hook.** Think of the difference between an assistant who can only *answer* your questions and one who can also *do the task*, look the information up, fill in the form, and book the appointment. The second assistant does not just advise; it takes action. An **AI agent** is that second kind.

**Build, tools.** A plain LLM is stuck with what it already knows and cannot reach the outside world. A **tool** is an outside capability we let the model call, for example: **search the web**, **run code**, **call an app**, or **query a database**. When the model can ask for these, it can pull in fresh information and make things happen, well beyond predicting text. Crucially, RAG-style retrieval is one such tool; an agent generalizes the idea to *many* tools and actions.

**Build, multi-step action.** What makes an LLM an **agent** is two things together: **using tools** and **planning across multiple steps** toward a goal. The loop looks like this: the model **decides** what to do next, **uses a tool**, **observes** the result, and then **repeats**, deciding again based on what it learned, until the goal is reached. So booking a dentist visit might be: decide to search for clinics, read the results, decide to check open times, then decide to fill the booking form, step after step.

**Honest note, agents are powerful but error-prone.** Because an agent takes real actions, a wrong step can have real consequences (sending the wrong message, deleting the wrong file). Agents need **guardrails**, limits and checks on what they may do, and important actions should be verified.

**Wrap-up for the whole topic.** LLMs are remarkable **pattern machines**, not all-knowing oracles. They predict plausible text, so **verify important outputs**. Use **RAG** to ground them in real facts and **tools/agents** to let them act, and keep a human eye on anything that matters. That combination, a strong language model plus retrieval and tools, is what modern AI assistants are built from.

**Recap.** An agent is an LLM that uses tools and plans multiple steps to act, not just chat: decide, act, observe, repeat. It is powerful but needs guardrails, and like all LLM output, its results deserve verification.

items.prompt: What makes an LLM an 'agent' rather than just a chatbot?
choices: It uses a larger font in its replies
It answers only yes-or-no questions
It can use tools and plan multiple steps to act toward a goal, not just produce text
It has a bigger context window and nothing else
items.explanation: An agent combines tool use with multi-step planning so it can take actions toward a goal, going beyond chatting. It is not about font size, yes/no answers, or context-window size alone, the defining traits are tools plus stepwise action.

items.prompt: In this context, what is a 'tool' that an agent can use?
choices: A physical screwdriver attached to the computer
A longer prompt template
Another copy of the same answer
An outside capability the model can call, such as web search, running code, calling an app, or querying a database
items.explanation: A tool is an external capability the model invokes, like searching the web, running code, calling an app, or querying a database, letting it reach beyond its own text. It is not a physical tool, a prompt template, or a duplicate answer.

items.prompt: What does the agent loop look like?
choices: It writes one reply and always stops immediately
It decides what to do, uses a tool, observes the result, and repeats until the goal is reached
It retrains itself on each step
It asks the user to perform every action manually
items.explanation: An agent cycles through deciding, using a tool, observing the outcome, and deciding again, looping toward the goal. It is not a single forced reply, it does not retrain itself each step, and the point is that the agent acts rather than asking the user to do everything.

items.prompt: The everyday analogy for an AI agent is best described as...
choices: An assistant who can not only answer but also do the steps, look it up, fill the form, and book the appointment
A vending machine that only dispenses one item
A printed encyclopedia on a shelf
A clock that only shows the time
items.explanation: An agent is like an assistant who takes action on your behalf, doing the steps rather than just advising. A vending machine, an encyclopedia, and a clock are all passive, they do not plan and act toward a goal the way an agent does.

items.prompt: Why do agents need 'guardrails'?
choices: Because they always refuse to do anything useful
Because guardrails make the model larger
Because they take real actions, so a wrong step can have real consequences, and limits and checks reduce the harm
Because without them the model cannot read the prompt
items.explanation: Since agents act in the real world, a mistaken action (a wrong message, a deleted file) can cause real damage, so guardrails set limits and checks. Guardrails are not about refusing all work, growing the model, or enabling prompt reading, they are about safety around actions.

items.prompt: How does an agent's use of tools relate to RAG from the previous lesson?
choices: They are unrelated; RAG has nothing to do with tools
An agent forbids any retrieval of documents
RAG and agents both retrain the model on every step
Retrieval (as in RAG) is one kind of tool; an agent generalizes the idea to many tools and multi-step actions
items.explanation: Fetching documents (RAG) is essentially one tool an agent might use; agents extend the concept to many tools plus planning. They are related, not opposed, an agent does not forbid retrieval, and neither RAG nor agents retrain the model on each step.

items.prompt: Which task most clearly shows an LLM acting as an agent rather than just chatting?
choices: Explaining in text what a dentist appointment is
Defining the word 'appointment'
Actually searching for clinics, checking open times, and filling in the booking form across several steps
Listing synonyms for the word 'book'
items.explanation: Carrying out a multi-step task with tools, search, check times, submit a form, is agent behavior, because it acts rather than merely describes. Explaining, defining, or listing synonyms are all just producing text, which is ordinary chat.

items.prompt: What is the honest, balanced takeaway about LLMs from this whole topic?
choices: LLMs are all-knowing oracles you can trust completely
LLMs are remarkable pattern machines, not oracles; verify important outputs, and combine them with retrieval (RAG) for facts and tools/agents for actions
LLMs should never be used for anything
LLMs already check every fact themselves, so verification is pointless
items.explanation: The grounded view is that LLMs are powerful pattern predictors that can be wrong, so you verify important results and pair them with RAG for facts and tools for actions. They are neither infallible oracles nor useless, and they do not self-verify facts, which is exactly why human checking matters.

items.prompt: Why is a plain LLM (no tools) limited, and how do tools address that?
choices: A plain LLM already controls the whole internet, so tools are pointless
Tools make the LLM forget how to write text
Tools only change the color of the answer
A plain LLM is stuck with what it already knows and cannot reach the outside world; tools let it fetch fresh information and make things happen
items.explanation: On its own an LLM cannot access live data or act, it only predicts text, so tools (search, code, apps, databases) extend it into the world. A plain LLM does not control the internet, tools do not erase its writing ability, and this is about capability, not color.

items.prompt: Pulling the modern LLM era together, which statement is most accurate?
choices: An LLM looks up truth in a database, RAG retrains it, and agents are just longer chatbots

An LLM predicts plausible text (and can hallucinate); fine-tuning/RLHF make it a helpful assistant; RAG feeds real documents into the prompt without retraining; and tools plus multi-step planning make it an agent that can act
Bigger prompts are always better, and agents never make mistakes
LLMs, RAG, and agents are all the exact same thing with different names
items.explanation: The accurate synthesis: LLMs generate plausible text and can hallucinate, fine-tuning/RLHF make them helpful, RAG grounds them by retrieving documents into the prompt (no retraining), and tools plus planning create acting agents. The other options repeat classic misconceptions, looking up truth in a database, RAG retraining, bigger-is-always-better, error-free agents, or conflating distinct ideas.
